constraints: Sum of N over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
Sum of Q over all test cases ≤ 3·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case: first line contains N, the number of nodes in the tree. The next N‑1 lines each contain two integers U and V, denoting an undirected edge. The next line contains Q, the number of queries. Each of the next Q lines contains two integers C and D. For each query let last be the answer of the previous query in the same test case (0 for the first query). Compute A = C xor last and X = D xor last. You have to output a node B (1 ≤ B ≤ N) such that when a depth‑first search defined in the statement starts from node A, the entry time tin[B] equals X.
content.outputFormat: For each query output a single integer B on its own line – the node whose entry time equals X when DFS starts from A.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The order in which DFS visits the vertices is [1,2,3][1, 2, 3][1,2,3], which means vertex 333 will have tin[3] = 2.
Test case 222:

Query 111: Here A=1A = 1A=1 and X=2X = 2X=2. The order in which DFS visits the vertices starting from 111 is [1,2,5,3,4][1, 2, 5, 3, 4][1,2,5,3,4], which means vertex 555 will have tin[5] = 2.
Query 222: The answer to the last query, last = 5. Thus, A=6⊕5=3A = 6 \oplus 5 = 3A=6⊕5=3 and X=1⊕5=4X = 1 \oplus 5 = 4X=1⊕5=4. The order in which DFS visits the vertices starting from 333 is [3,5,1,2,4][3, 5, 1, 2, 4][3,5,1,2,4], which means vertex 444 will have tin[4] = 4.

solution.approach:
For every directed edge (u → v) and for every node considered as a root (state (u, parent) where parent = 0 means u is the start of DFS) we define the *next* state – the state visited immediately after the first visit of u in the DFS. The next state can be computed recursively:
1. From state (u, p) look at the sorted adjacency list of u.
2. The first neighbour w ≠ p becomes the next node; the next state is (w, u).
3. If no such neighbour exists, the DFS backtracks – the next state is the next state of (p, u).
4. For the artificial parent 0 there is no backtrack.
Because the tree has no cycles, this definition yields a functional graph of O(N) states (root state for each node + two directed states for each edge). We compute the next state for every state once using memoisation (DFS on the functional graph). After that we build a binary‑lifting table up to log₂N for fast jumps.
Answering a query (A, X) is then a matter of starting from the root state (A, 0) and jumping X steps using the lifting table; the node stored in the resulting state is the answer. Each query is answered in O(log N) time.
The preprocessing per test case costs O(N log N) (sorting adjacency lists and building the lifting table). Memory consumption is O(N log N) for the lifting table plus O(N) for the graph representation.
solution.timeComplexity: Preprocessing per test case: O(N log N). Each query: O(log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N log N) for binary lifting tables and O(N) for adjacency and state mappings.
